expectations.text: Recommends moving the orchestration (manager call + persist + map) out of the controller and into an OrderAppService that the controller delegates to
expectations.evidence: Use-case orchestration (calling OrderManager, inserting via the repository, mapping to a DTO) must live in an application service - not a controller... Move the body into OrderAppService.CreateAsync(CreateOrderDto input).

expectations.text: States the controller should inject only the application-service interface (e.g. IOrderAppService) and that injecting IRepository / OrderManager / IObjectMapper / ICurrentUser into a controller is wrong
expectations.evidence: It should be a thin pass-through... private readonly IOrderAppService _orderAppService; No repository, no domain service, no mapper in the controller. Drop ICurrentUser from the controller.

expectations.text: Replaces the imperative _currentUser.IsInRole("sales") check with declarative [Authorize(...)] referencing an ABP permission constant (e.g. [Authorize(OrderPermissions.Create)] or similar) — NOT [Authorize(Roles="sales")] (role strings) and NOT a bare [Authorize] without a policy/permission
expectations.evidence: [Authorize(OrderPermissions.Create)] public virtual Task<OrderDto> CreateAsync(CreateOrderDto input). Hardcoded role checks is a Never-Use anti-pattern; use permission-based authorization instead.

expectations.text: Shows the corrected action body as a single delegating expression to the AppService (or a one-liner equivalent) — no business logic in the controller

Applies [Authorize(OrderPermissions.Create)] (or equivalent permission-based attribute) at the application-service layer — on the AppService class or the CreateAsync method itself — NOT only on the controller. The AppService is the canonical authz boundary in ABP because other callers (background jobs, integration services, event handlers) routing through the AppService also get the check
expectations.evidence: Drop the class-level [Authorize] on OrderController and move authorization to [Authorize(OrderPermissions.Create)] on OrderAppService.CreateAsync. That single attribute then covers: Direct in-process callers (other modules, background jobs, tests)...
